speaker: interviewer
text: The date is August 10, 1995. My name is Gloria Perlman Schneider. We are interviewing Matthew Guttman in Queens, New York. Mr. Guttman, you were just saying about liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was May 5, 1945. Before, the will to live-- the word liberation wasn't that-- it was a very hard possibility. We couldn't believe, really, that it was going to happen, I count, because I knew for a fact there were lots of places where they have liquidated the camps, killing all the inmates. So whenever it is going to be the ultimate hour, they can do whatever they like with us. So at the end of the machine gun, everything perishes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't want to believe. But one day, being in Gunskirchen, I see a guard who was-- because they were grave diggers. They were digging big, big graves. Also, lining up the people-- two people were in the grave lining up, the people, one with a head here and the other one with a head there, that should be more production. I saw the guard reading a paper, a German paper-- Hitler kaput. And [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- everything is over. I said to myself, what? I cannot believe.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am looking again with the corner of my eye. I don't want to show him this. I am happy about it-- Hitler kaput. And [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was the last-- where they taking the dead bodies to the grave, May the 5th, Friday, 1945. And we hear shootings, but not like with guns. It was shooting like with [INAUDIBLE], you know, like-- like heavier guns than machine guns, heavier. And everything was towards the woods. We came with a dead body to the grave. They ask me what happens there in the camp. They thought the inevitable. I says, I don't know, because I didn't see nothing. Slowly, I see by coming back from there-- usually, we used to have escort, you know, SS. Now I see and old SS man. He's going with-- he's going behind us. And we were talking Hungarian language, you know, with friends of mine. And he was also from the Hungarian territory, but he was a German [NON-ENGLISH]. And he joined the German army. He was an old person. And I see he takes out bread, and he starts cutting with his pocket knife pieces of bread. And he throws it on the floor. He wasn't allowed to give us. He was afraid he shouldn't be caught giving us bread. And we jumped. Everybody was fighting for the morsels. Everybody was fighting over the bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they wanted to take away the bread from him. So he took the gun, you know, showing that, I'm going to shoot you. But he didn't mean it. And then he starts talking. He says, one of these days, you are going to be liberated, sooner than later. But what is going to be with me? For three months, I didn't even hear about my family. They are somewhere in Czechoslovakia. I don't know where they are and what it's going to be with me. Oh, Herr Commandant, you are a nice person. We are going to say witness for you. You know, I see this-- I-- I can't let myself go with him, because he's a nice guy now. Maybe he knows-- who knows how many people he-- how many dead bodies he has on his-- on his consciousness? So anyway, but we are coming in. The SS disappear. And now we are coming without escort, coming in the camp when the camp is the same thing. But we don't see no Germans. Now, I said to myself, oh, there is no escort? I'm not going to go, because they're going to say that I ran away. I tried to run away, and they're going to kill me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then looking towards the entrance to the camp, I see a jeep coming in with a Red Cross flag-- an American flag, but I don't know. I never saw an American flag in my life. And a captain gets out-- no, he doesn't get out. He climbs on his feet on the-- on the jeep. And he says [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He started to say German. He was wrong. He was-- OK. I knew what he meant to say. I forgave him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, you, Jews, everything is free for you. He wanted to say that you are all free. He goes, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. We look at the other. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Is that possible? But I don't see no SS. I don't see no SS. I said to myself, is that possible that we should be free?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away, people jump to the food magazines, to the warehouses. They start taking out bread, margarine. Because it was their warehouses. We didn't have no warehouse. And ammunition warehouses, they start shooting in the air. It was a crazy house. They didn't know what to do with that, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That word-- liberty, freedom. They start to cry, the people. They didn't want to believe themselves. Then I see what is going on here. People were jumping into the warehouse through the window because the door was locked. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One was jumping, and the other one snuck. If somebody came out ready with two or three breads from there, outside the rest were jumping in. And they were tearing the bread, you know, in crumbs. And it fell on the floor-- nobody, except maybe the birds, had any value of it. But you, nobody had not even a little chunk, nothing. I look. I said to myself, oh no. If I am free, I don't want to die. You know, people were dying. They were eating sugar from the bags with their skinny stomach, with their [INAUDIBLE]. They were eating, and they were-- excuse me for expressing myself-- the dysentery puss was going out from his behind and not anything else. And they were dying on the place. Looking on that, like that, I said, oh no, not me. I started walking towards the exit of the camp. Because the American captain, he just came, he said what he had to say, turned around, and he went.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Americans left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. He went back to his detachment. Because I'm coming to the highway. The highway was about two kilometers from the woods from the camp. I'm coming to the highway, a bit lost on the highway. It was no cement, asphalt like here. It was summer, spring. I see big dust.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, what the hell's going on? And the trucks-- one after the other after the other one after the other-- American army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, on the highway, you cannot walk, because the trucks are going. So I went on the side of the highway, in the ditch-- like they're digging ditches, the waters would run down from the highway. So I thought to myself, I'm going to take the ditch. I'm going to go alongside the highway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I walk. That American soldier saw Jude, the yellow Star of David-- Jude. They started throwing me cigarettes, chocolate, and whatever. And this army was 90% blacks. The officers were white. Oh, boy-- I start eating chocolate. I said to myself, Matthew, you're not supposed to do that. That is no good, either.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walk 6 kilometers. I'm coming to a city called Wels, upper Austria. There, the highway is cut off. Nobody can enter the city, because the action is still on in the city. They didn't capture all the Nazis. And a stray bullet-- you can catch a stray bullet. And I said to myself, OK. The patrol, what stayed guard there, they were about 10 guys, they start talking to me. I don't understand a word of English. He says, [NON-ENGLISH]. I speak to them German. They don't understand me. Whatever they say, I don't understand them. Behind there, it was a bakery. An Austrian-- he's formerly American citizen. He used to be an American before. He spoke some English, and of course, German. They're bringing him out to talk to me. They didn't see yet such an animal with a Star of David Jude.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we talk to him. He translates. He's asking me-- he says, where are you from? I say Gunskirchen. That was six kilometers from there. They are asking him. He says-- I says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? I said, I am an inmate from the concentration camp. I'm a Jew. He says, you know that you cannot enter the city? Where are you going? I says, I don't know. I just go. I was 25 years old. And I wanted to go prospecting. He says, you'll have to sleep here till the morning. In the morning, he says the action is going to be over. Then you can go wherever you want. I said, how can I sleep here? I'm full of lice. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- one of the patrol-- black guys, a Sergeant-- he was maybe 6 and 1/2 feet or 7 feet tall-- he takes me by my hand, I was shivering of my knees, you know, a skeleton. He takes me across the street toward the [GERMAN]. It was an army barrack, formerly German army. The Wehrmacht had their barracks there. Now it was full with German prisoners with Americans captured them, and they throw them in there. And by the windows, and by the gates, by the entrances everywhere was machine guns. And taking care of the prisoners. He takes me in there. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Push the button. [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now she locked herself in. So you were saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: He took me to the [GERMAN] there. They were full of prisoners there. And he tells me I should undress, takes me to the bathroom-- where the prisoners came to urinate. He takes me, he gives me a shower, he washes me, that black sergeant.

speaker: interviewer
text: He shaves off all my hairy parts of my body, from the top to the bottom. And he's asking the Germans that's there-- he says, where is the clothing warehouse? He showed him there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big lock on the door. He takes his gun out, you know, he shoots in the lock, a whole round into it. The lock tears apart. He goes in. He says to me in English, [GIBBERISH]. This was what I understood. That much what I understood English. I knew what he meant. I shall go, and I should get dressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, they were German clothing, the German army clothing. So I thought to myself, why should I take on-- on soldier clothing? I'm going to take, you know, an officer's clothing. I'm going to look like a mensch. When I came out from there, you would swear that I am Goebbels. I am Hitler's friend. So I came out and went back to the bakery. He takes a canister of DDT power from the jeep. He delouses me. He sprays me from here, from all over the sleeves, everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And really, he did a very, very good job. Because they didn't bite no more. And he takes from the jeep one of the rations-- some butter. From the bakery, we get some bread. He smears me some butter on the bread. They had in the army-- I don't know if you know about it-- they had, the American army, they had some chocolate milk. It was like a can of chocolate milk. And with gunpowder, you lit the gunpowder, and that gunpowder heated up the chocolate milk. It was boiling. As a matter of fact, they had to open up. Because if not, the chocolate milk would have spread around. He takes out a chocolate milk from there. He take out the chocolate milk. And of course, he's giving me. Now he says you sleep here. He goes back to his post. He's giving me a piece of paper that he's Sergeant so-and-so from this battalion and this and this and this and this. I have disinfected this Jew from Gunskirchen, and I deloused him. And I tore off the yellow Star of David, only for-- as a souvenir. But he went away to his post. I said to myself, oh, like hell am I going to sleep here. I see a bicycle there on the side of the street in the ditch, but not by the post. I didn't go by the post. I go around the houses. I take this bicycle, because some officer or some German threw it away. Some German soldier threw it away. I picked it up. I'm getting on the bicycle, and into the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's getting dark slowly, and I'm sorry that I left. And of course, bullets are whistling near my ear. How many times? And I was ducking in the places. The army, the American army-- hey, who are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took out the piece of paper and I showed it to him. I kept it, you know, under my hat. I showed it to him. OK. So I went. I'm coming to a place that was called the Hitler Platz in Wels. I didn't know where I am, because I was a stranger in a strange town. An old, old old Austrian says to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- He thought that I am a German. [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you going? Who are you? He saw that nobody touches me. [SPEAKING GERMAN], I am a Jew, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Where are you going? I don't know. I was sorry already, between me and you and the lamppost, that I left. He says, come with me. I'm going. He shows me a building. He says, go up there on the third floor. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That was an SS officer's home, a club of the SS officers.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are going up there. They just captured all the Germans from there. He says, you'll have everything what your heart's desire. Coming up the third floor, leave my bicycle downstairs. Coming up there, a big noise. I try to open the door. The door opens, but a big noise, and the smoke from cigarettes was so thick you could cut it with a knife. I'm running down. I was afraid, you know, maybe somebody was there. Liberty is-- goodbye, liberty. But nobody's running after me. Ah. I'm going back. Open the door slowly. Of course, it was a radio barking-- where-- which Ally were, entering, where they are capturing, where are they going, what are they doing, and which towns are occupied already from the Allies, and which from the Russians, and so on. I said to myself, hey. I'm going there. I found everything best. I'm taking off. There were closets. There were rooms-- five officers to a room. Everyone had a bath, everyone had a-- a closet for clothes and things like that. I'm going prospecting between the closets there. I see civilian clothing. I said to myself, mazel tov. Now I'm going to take off Goebbels's clothes. And I open the window. I'm throwing them out. Here, they should have it-- into the Hitler Platz. And I-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] I am going to dress myself in civilian clothing. OK. I had everything there. It was food, it was to eat, it was to drink, it was everything. It was raw apple, and raw onions, and dry onions and, dry apple. I started to bite in the onion, because I didn't eat in such a long time. I bit into the apple. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks God. The next day, I see on the street that the Americans are capturing, you know, the skeletons. [BACKGROUND NOISE] They're capturing the former skeletons. They're capturing them on the street like the dog catcher catches dogs. Because they were infesting the whole area full of typhoid fever. And they were catching them on the street, and they were taking them into the [GERMAN]. They emptied out from the prisoners, and they made hospitals out of them. Even from garages, they made hospitals, put in army cots, a sick person to an army cot. American army doctors-- they mobilized the doctors from the city, the Austrian doctors. They brought in milk. They try, you know, to feed, you know, the hungry. You know-- they-- More people died after the war, maybe, than before-- during the war. Because Americans weren't prepared, you know, with such a kind of material, because they were eating all this [GERMAN], this-- the pork, 65% lard and 35% meat. They were licking it up. And that's the way they died with it. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you-- what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, I happened to find out on a list my sister is alive. She wrote to my uncle. She didn't find me in the list. She wrote to my uncle that she is here in this [GERMAN]. That is a hospital in Wels, the same city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to bring the change with lists for survivors, that you should-- could find me and I find you, and so on-- brothers and sisters and cousins and uncles, and so on and so on. And she found only my uncle. She didn't find me. We are going to the hospital, my uncle and I. We're going to the hospital. She was in typhoid fever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three weeks later, I try to bring her home. But she tried me out-- what am I going to do now? Am I-- what is my intentions? I said, to go home. Did you see? Did you know that I am alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know that you are alive. Maybe somebody else from seven children. Oh, I don't know about our parents. My parents were 45 and 47 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: But somebody else. Where can we meet after the war? Only home. No, no, no. There is nobody. She told me that in Auschwitz, she was chosen to the right, and the rest were all going to the left. So whoever went to the left went straight to the gas chambers, and that is the finale. So one morning, I'm going to bring her home. I'm supposed to bring her into the camp already, because she was already recuperating and everything was all right. I didn't see her. She wasn't already in. Her next bed, a friend of hers tells me she left this morning, 5 o'clock. I says, where to? She went away. They're supposed to be her husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I never knew that she was married. And she never told me. I used to go to the hospital every day to see her, and I was a nobody. And she didn't tell me. And she didn't want to go home. She said no, no, no, there is nobody, nobody, nobody. We have to go somewhere else. This godforsaken country, we don't want to go back. She disappeared, because she was afraid that I'm going to force her to come home. I went. On the way, I found the tracks where she was going, but I didn't know her married name. Wherever I came, they asked me what is his name. In Europe, you cannot go on your maiden name. Everything goes under my husband's name. Mrs. Guttman-- oh, that were if you were a girl, so you went on your family name. The end of the story was I came to south of France, Vichy. They told me that there is a camp where they're going to America. I stayed there for about two weeks. And nothing doing. I didn't see her. Then they told me that the Jewish agency in Rome, from Palestine, where they were fighting alongside the British, I should go there, because they're making Aliyah Bet transports, you know, illegally to Palestine. I went there. There I was seven weeks, and nothing doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you looking for her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was looking for her. I went to the Jewish agency, to the headquarters. And they asked me right away, what is her name? I told her her maiden name, but how should I know? She's supposed to be married to a Greek druggist, Jewish druggist, also a former inmate in the concentration camp. So I lost track. I never found her. I wrote to the Red Cross in Switzerland, and nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghiselle. The end of the-- you're finished?
